explanation.text: The sentence says Nellie "set out" one year after requesting the trip, and the next details describe her traveling east across the Atlantic. This context shows that the phrase means she began her journey.
Nellie's goal is to travel around the world in fewer than eighty days. Taking only one bag supports that goal because carrying less allows her to move quickly.

The author first describes Nellie leaving, writing during the trip, and crossing Europe and Asia, then tells of her arrival in San Francisco and return to New York. This sequence shows the events in the order they happened.
Nellie invents a new style of "journalism" by reporting what she sees, thinks, and feels to her newspaper's readers. That description indicates that journalism means writing for newspapers.

The map traces Nellie's completed route from the New York area through Europe and Asia, across the Pacific, and back across the United States in seventy-two days. This completed journey supports the statement that she met her challenge to circle the world in fewer than eighty days.

Nellie refuses limits placed on women, completes the journey faster than the proposed goal, and proves herself competent and resourceful. These details support the main idea that a woman demonstrated she could accomplish what people assumed only a man could do.

King Tiger orders Borneo to give him food and threatens an attack if its animals refuse; later, he proposes making the same demand of the elephants. His threats show that "demand" means to insist on receiving something.

Mouse Deer's thoughts tell the reader that Borneo has no tigers at all, much less a tiger king. The visiting tigers do not know this, which explains why his promise to carry their message deceives them.

Although Mouse Deer trembles, he invents a fearsome king who sharpens his claws between mountains and welcomes a fight. His convincing story shows both the cleverness of his plan and the bravery required to face the tigers.
Mouse Deer prances only after his trick has astonished the tigers and sent them swimming away. This lively movement reveals that he feels pleased and satisfied with how the encounter ended.

Mouse Deer borrows a large porcupine quill and presents it as a whisker from Borneo's supposedly enormous tiger king. Because the quill frightens King Tiger, that choice causes him to redirect his demands to the Island of the Elephants.

After staring at the enormous "whisker," King Tiger abruptly claims he has been thinking and suggests attacking a different island. This excuse reveals that he is scared of Borneo's supposed king but does not want his followers to see his fear.
